text: This is a case of a sixty-three-year-old male who admitted to the ER with the complaint of left lower quadrant pain. He had a history of three episodes of diverticulitis and no history of surgery. The abdomen was tender and there was defense in the left lower quadrant as well as rebound tenderness. In blood tests, high white blood cells count and increased CRP were detected. Abdominal CT revealed pelvic abscess and suspected perforation due to diverticulitis. This CT scan shows the diverticulitis of descending and sigmoid columns and also pelvic abscess secondary to the perforation.

This is a case of a sixty-three-year-old male who admitted to the ER with the complaint of left lower quadrant pain. He had a history of three episodes of diverticulitis and no history of surgery. The abdomen was tender and there was defense in the left lower quadrant as well as rebound tenderness. In blood tests, high white blood cells count and increased CRP were detected. Abdominal CT revealed pelvic abscess and suspected perforation due to diverticulitis. This CT scan shows the diverticulitis of descending and sigmoid columns and also pelvic abscess secondary to the perforation.

The surgeon begins the procedure with sharp dissection of dense intra-abdominal adhesions, focusing on the mesocolon. Pus visibly flows from the pelvic abscess and is aspirated using a suction device.

The surgeon begins the procedure with sharp dissection of dense intra-abdominal adhesions. The mesocolon is being carefully dissected, and pus visibly flows out from the pelvic abscess, which is then aspirated.

The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries, applying purple hemoclips to ligate them separately due to their large diameters. The vessels are divided using an energy device. Told's fascia is carefully divided, and the pelvic area appears cleared after abscess drainage. The rectosigmoid colon is prepared for resection.

The surgeon dissects the left colic and sigmoidal arteries, applying purple hemoclips to each vessel. Due to the large diameters, the arteries are ligated separately before being divided with an energy device.
The surgeon carefully divides Toldt's fascia, revealing a cleared pelvic area following abscess drainage. The rectosigmoid colon is prepared for resection.

The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a six-centimeter segment. A pharyngeal incision is made, and the specimen is extracted from the abdomen. The proximal resection border is set at the middle of the descending colon. A circular stapler is placed at the proximal end of the colon, which is then reintroduced into the abdomen. An end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis is performed using a 31-millimeter circular stapler, followed by an air leak test to verify the anastomosis integrity.

The surgeon completes the distal resection using a GIA stapler, creating a six-centimeter incision. A pharyngeal incision is made, and the specimen is extracted from the abdomen. The proximal resection border is set at the middle of the descending colon. A circular stapler is placed at the proximal end of the colon, which is then reintroduced into the abdomen. An end-to-end brictocolic anastomosis is performed using a thirty-one millimeter circular stapler, followed by an air leak test to check the anastomosis integrity.
